report_name: AI-Powered Phishing Hits New Levels of Sophistication, Bypassing MFA

threat_actors.name: TA4903
threat_actors.description: A threat actor group using 'device code phishing' to bypass multi-factor authentication in large-scale phishing campaigns.

attack_patterns.name: Spearphishing Link
attack_patterns.description: Users receive an AI-generated phishing email with a malicious link that initiates the device code authentication flow.

attack_patterns.name: Multi-Factor Authentication
attack_patterns.description: Attackers use the device code phishing technique to trick users into authorizing a malicious application, bypassing MFA and gaining persistent access via a refresh token.

attack_patterns.name: Valid Accounts
attack_patterns.description: After a successful device code phishing attack, the attacker gains persistent access to the user's account using a valid refresh token granted by the identity platform.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
attack_patterns.description: The device code phishing attack results in the attacker's application being granted a refresh token and an access token, which are functionally equivalent to session cookies for persistent access.

attack_patterns.name: Phishing
attack_patterns.description: Threat actors leverage Generative AI to craft hyper-realistic, grammatically flawless, and highly personalized spear-phishing emails to impersonate trusted entities.

tools.name: Generative AI
tools.description: Used by attackers to automate the creation of highly convincing, personalized, and grammatically flawless spear-phishing emails at scale.

tools.name: EvilTokens
tools.description: A Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) kit that provides the tools and infrastructure to carry out sophisticated, MFA-bypassing phishing campaigns.

tools.name: Tycoon
tools.description: A Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) kit that provides templates, infrastructure, and tutorials to carry out device code phishing attacks that bypass MFA.

identities.name: HMRC
identities.description: Her Majesty's Revenue and Customs (HMRC), a UK government agency, was impersonated in AI-powered phishing campaigns.

identities.name: NHS
identities.description: The National Health Service (NHS) in the UK was impersonated in AI-powered phishing campaigns.

infrastructure.name: Device Code Phishing Infrastructure
infrastructure.description: Attacker-controlled web pages used to initiate the device authentication flow and present the user with a device code as part of an MFA-bypass phishing attack.

indicators.name: Suspicious Azure AD Application Consent
indicators.pattern:
title: Suspicious Azure AD Application Consent
id: f1a2b3c4-d5e6-4789-a0b1-c2d3e4f5a6b7
status: experimental
description: >
  Detects when a user grants consent to a new enterprise application in Azure AD.
  This can be a sign of a successful device code phishing attack where a user is tricked into authorizing a malicious application.
  Investigation should focus on applications with unusual names, unverified publishers, or high-risk permissions.
references:
  - https://blog.usecure.io/phishing-attack-alert-2026-new-ai-email-scams
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-21
tags:
  - attack.t1556.006
  - attack.credential_access
level: medium
logsource:
  product: azure
  service: auditlogs
detection:
  selection:
    OperationName: 'Consent to application'
    Result: 'success'
  filter_microsoft:
    InitiatedBy.app.displayName: 'Microsoft Admin Console'
  condition: selection and not filter_microsoft
indicators.description: Detects when a user grants consent to a new enterprise application in Azure AD, which could indicate a successful device code phishing attack. Focus on applications with unusual names, unverified publishers, or high-risk permissions.

indicators.name: Potential Device Code Phishing Activity
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Potential Device Code Phishing Activity
id: e8d7c6b5-a493-4210-b1c2-d3e4f5a6b7c8
status: experimental
description: >
  Detects network traffic to the legitimate Microsoft device code authentication page (microsoft.com/devicecode).
  This activity is often benign but is a key step in device code phishing attacks.
  Alerts should be correlated with other phishing indicators, such as clicks from suspicious emails.
references:
  - https://blog.usecure.io/phishing-attack-alert-2026-new-ai-email-scams
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-21
tags:
  - attack.t1566.002
  - attack.initial_access
level: informational
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    cs-host: 'microsoft.com'
    c-uri-stem: '/devicecode'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: Detects traffic to the legitimate Microsoft device code authentication page. While often benign, this traffic can be an indicator of a device code phishing attempt when correlated with other suspicious activity, such as a user clicking a link from an untrusted email.
